data.title: Dividend Cut Alert: 2 BDCs With Unsustainable Yields
data.description:
There is a general consensus that the BDC (BIZD) yields are slowly but surely marching towards the chopping block. The key driver that is pushing the BDCs in that direction is the Fed's policy. As we have already evidenced it on the ground from the several cuts that have been made since mid-2024, whenever rates go down, the BDC earnings tend to follow.

The issue here, which really forms such a pessimistic consensus, is the combination of the following factors (I'm listing my Top 3, but obviously there are more):

  1. Interest rates are headed downwards.
  2. The BDC sector average base dividend coverage is 100%.
  3. Many BDCs have exhausted their balance sheets (i.e., target level leverage achieved from which funding new growth would be too risky).

This is a clear recipe for experiencing dividend cuts across the board. Very hard to argue against that.

Having said that, it is clearly not that, say, in Q1 2025 all the BDCs that have their dividend coverage levels tight will be collectively announcing lower dividends. Far from it.

We have to understand that the average includes many outliers, both on the positive and negative ends.

For example, some BDCs carry such ammunition as a) equity investment exposures that, if monetized, would easily cover dividends for quite some time; b) overcapitalized balance sheets that could accommodate leverage-driven growth; and c) P/NAV premiums, which could facilitate accretive equity issuances that would also help drive growth and thus fight back the earnings pressure. So, this is about the positive outliers.

Similarly, there is the negative end of the spectrum, which is where we will very likely see first cracks and dividend cuts.

In the article below, I will elaborate on two such BDCs, which, in my view, stand first in line in front of the chopping block.

#1 - PNNT

PennantPark Investment Corporation (PNNT) is a relatively standard BDC, which focuses on first-lien lending. Yet, the financial parameters are nowhere close to being standard. Let me unpack this.

First, PNNT has been dealing with declining earnings generation since late 2024 - i.e., each quarter has brought negative surprises. For example, in the most recent report, PNNT delivered core net investment income per share of $0.15, primarily due to a lower base rate and some spread compression.

Second, if we put the $0.15 per share figure in the context of a quarterly base dividend of $0.24 per share, we will arrive at a notable problem. Namely, the base dividend coverage level is among the worst in the BDC world and currently sits at 62.5%. It means that just a bit more than half of what PNNT has to pay out in dividends gets covered by earnings. The rest comes from leverage or received capital from loan repayment, which does not get reinvested back in growth.

Third, the actual cash dividend coverage is even worse if we adjust for the PIK factor, which accounts for ~14% of the total net investment income.

Fourth, theoretically, one might argue that PNNT could tap into its balance sheet to fuel additional loan funding, which would then in turn lift up the core net investment income figure. The reality is that this argument simply does not hold true since the leverage stands at 1.6x, which is one of the highest levels in the sector.

Fifth, and this is a bit more technical, a notable chunk of PNNT's earnings stems from the underlying joint ventures, which are not consolidated in the books. The issue is that within these structures there lies an even higher leverage level than what we can see on PNNT's balance sheet. For example, the largest joint venture generates an NII yield of 17%, which is significantly above the yield that PNNT extracts from its consolidated assets. So, if this JV starts to experience non-accruals, the consequences might be huge given the magnified effects that are associated with relatively aggressive leverage. Granted, it might not happen now, but sooner or later there simply should be some non-accrual pressures, which is completely logical for any BDC/private credit vehicle.

In a nutshell, it is hard to imagine how PNNT could keep the existing dividend intact for much longer than one or two quarters.

#2 - OCSL

Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL) is also an externally managed, first-lien-focused BDC with very little equity focus. The underlying core exposures to the direct lending factors are similar to PNNT.

As opposed to PFTL, OCSL has suffered some pain already this year in terms of delivering a steep dividend cut and receiving equity injection (support) from its private equity owner, Oaktree Capital Management.

The issue that OCSL faced was inflating non-accruals, which, coupled with the general headwinds (i.e., lower base rates and tighter spreads), inflicted significant damage to the NII per share generation.

In order to halt the NAV-funded dividend distribution process (which is currently the case with PNNT), the management decided to cut the dividend, inject fresh equity into the system, and introduce a temporary fee waiver.

While all of this did indeed provide an immediate benefit to OCSL's financials, the situation after the support package continued to worsen. In other words, OCSL recorded new non-accruals and faced the same pressures as most BDCs, mainly from lower base rates. This is why, on a YTD basis, OCSL is down ~16% (on a price return level).

Currently, the picture seems somewhat positive if we look at the leverage. OCSL has a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.02x, which could help the BDC generate fresh NII per share flows that could offset the pressures from forthcoming interest rate cuts (including the lagged effects that come from the most recent one).

Having said that, I think that OCSL is very close to cutting its dividend again.

I see three clear reasons.

First, the base dividend coverage has declined to 102.5%. Yet, we have to consider here a) ~11% PIK income of the total NII, b) slight support coming from waived incentive fees (this will disappear in 2026), and c) further headwinds from lower base rates. Even if OCSL steps up its loan funding game, I doubt that it will be enough to achieve a fully cash-funded dividend

Second, in the most recent quarter, OCSL did a solid job on the non-accrual front. However, given the historical dynamics, it would be only prudent if we assigned a decent probability for OCSL experiencing new non-accruals. If that happens, the earnings will for sure fall below the dividend level.

Third, and this is strongly related to the previous point, OCSL has ~23% of its portfolio placed in software businesses, which, in my view, are more exposed to AI risk than other, non-cyclical industries, which we, for example, in the Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) case, can see constitute ~95% of its portfolio. Now, if we put this together with the fact that in Q4 2025, OCSL's median portfolio company median EBITDA dropped by ~7% and leverage increased by 0.01x, I think that you will agree with me that it would make perfect sense to factor in some headwinds from software-driven non-accruals going forward.

In a nutshell, the story for OCLS's dividend cut is just as straightforward as for PNNT. The difference, however, is that OCSL could potentially keep the existing distribution level as it has some gas in the tank (leverage) to fuel portfolio growth, which could at least partially offset the earnings pressures that lie ahead. Plus, it does not have a massively underfunded dividend, as in PNNT's case. So, my base case scenario is that we will see OCSL slashing its dividend in mid-2026, where the event could happen sooner if OCSL reports another 2%+ of new non-accruals.

data.title: Academy Sports & Outdoors: Upgrade To Buy On Better Fundamental Outlook
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Academy Sports & Outdoors (ASO) previously with a hold rating stance because I struggled to see a clear catalyst that would cause an upwards re-rating. My view has changed after the latest print. The core business is proving far more resilient than I expected, the customer mix is shifting in the right direction, and the new-store opening strategy is showing tangible proof it works.

3Q 2025 Earnings Review

Starting with revenue, ASO delivered net sales of $1.38 billion, implying a growth of 3% y/y. Comparable sales growth [CSG] came in at -0.9% y/y, which was slightly below consensus expectations (note: BBG FY26 is actually FY25), but context matters here. The poorer-than-expected performance was mainly due to the ammunition category (part of the Outdoor segment), which saw a tough comparison to last year’s. This category alone saw a 130 bps headwind. If we back that out, the core business actually saw CSG of 0.4%. The other categories did well. Sports & Recreation grew 6% y/y; Apparel grew 3% y/y; and Footwear and Outdoors grew 2% y/y. As for profitability, I was worried previously that heavier promotions would hurt margins, but the opposite happened. Gross margin expanded 170 bps to 35.7%, and that stronger margin profile flowed directly to earnings. Adj. net income grew 9.6% y/y to $77.3 million, and adj. EPS grew 16.3% y/y to $1.14.

A Structural Shift in the Consumer Base

ASO’s exposure to households earning <$50k has been a concern of mine since my last update, and this cohort of customers continues to see decline again, down mid-single digits y/y. But the one new datapoint that got me to be less negative about this exposure is that broader mix shift. Traffic from households earning >$100k grew high single digits y/y, and this group now makes up ~40% of total sales. Historically, sales were split almost evenly across low-, middle-, and high-income tiers. This mix shift effectively improves ASO’s overall consumer profile and inherently makes it more resilient because lower-income consumers are the most sensitive to inflation and tend to pull back quickly. Higher-income shoppers, on the other hand, behave differently. They have more disposable income and can afford more discretionary spending. That is not to say they will splurge whenever they can. The current macro dynamic still “forces” them to be more value conscious, and I think ASO is in a sweet spot here, where it is delivering better value relative to specialty retailers (management said this during the call). They even noted this trend has been consistent for four quarters, which tells me it’s an ongoing trend.

One interesting point I took away from the call was that once these high-income shoppers walk into ASO’s doors, they aren’t just buying national brands; they’re actually buying ASO’s private labels like Magellan and Freely. This changes the narrative entirely because it creates a stickier relationship (if the product is good, chances are customers will come back to ASO to purchase again since it is exclusive to ASO).

For this entire chain of events to work, the one thing that matters is the “hook,” which ASO leverages its partnerships with premium brands to lure in these high-income shoppers. For instance, the brand's partnership with Nike is one of the key drivers of traffic, which has continued to gain momentum. The combined Nike and Jordan business grew high single digits y/y in Q3. Looking ahead, given the success of the rollout of Jordan into 145 doors (which has been especially strong), management plans to broaden both apparel and footwear access in 2026. These new products should capture the attention of the targeted high-income shoppers, as they want to continue buying their beloved brands but at a better value price.

Management expects the same pattern to hold, in that once these shoppers come for the Jordan brands, they would also look for Magellan outerwear or Freely apparel. There is somewhat of a flywheel effect ongoing here. The strong sell-through with Nike shows ASO is a solid brand partner to have, which makes it easier to secure additional premium brands, which gives it more “hooks” to lure in shoppers and therefore increase purchase of high-margin private label goods. In fact, we’re already seeing early signs of this happening with ASO adding the Carhartt brand and also specialized assortments like World Cup soccer balls.

The New Store Growth Engine Is Finally Working

One point I made in September was that ASO may have found a viable and scalable model to restart new store openings. It appears that I am right. Stores opened between 2022 and 2024 are not only performing well, but performance is also actually accelerating. In Q3, these older stores saw CSG of high single digits, a sharp acceleration from the low single digits in Q1 2025 and the mid-single digits in Q2 2025. To give a sense of how impressive this is. Consider that, historically, once a store hits its 14th month (enters the comp base calculation), it starts to contribute positively to the blended CSG figure. But for these 2022 to 2024 stores, management noted they contributed ~50 bps to the total comp in Q3, which is more than the entire blended CSG.

The impact will be even more apparent as more of these stores roll into the comp base, and with ASO planning to open another 20 to 25 stores in fiscal 2026, blended CSG should continue to go up for the foreseeable future.

Valuation

It makes sense that the market re-rated ASO’s valuation up from the average of ~7.5x NTM PE to 8.7x as of the time of writing. The fundamentals have clearly improved. The elevated inventory situation did not turn into a margin headwind; the shift in consumer profile is very positive; and ASO's new unit growth strategy should continue to drive CSG increase.

Management guidance for FY26 implies adj. EPS to fall by low single digits at the midpoint, but I do expect earnings growth to accelerate, as Q3 showed us the potential (adj. EPS was up ~16%). The consensus estimate for ASO in FY28 is ~$8.2, implying a low-teens CAGR, which I think is fine looking at Q3 numbers and the fundamentals. Say that ASO trades at 8.7x FY28 adj. EPS; this implies a share price of $71.

Risk

The biggest near-term risk is price elasticity. If the macro situation gets a lot worse from here, say it dips into a recession, overall traffic may fall by a lot more, and that will hurt ASO's ability to accelerate earnings growth. One thing to also be cautious about is the quality of traffic. Management did note that consumer engagement remains highly episodic, wherein they engage primarily during promotional events. If the traffic is proven to be non-organic (always driven by promotions), it would hurt the long-term growth outlook, as consumers may form the habit of waiting for promotions.

Conclusion

I upgrade to a buy rating for ASO because the underlying fundamentals now point to a healthier growth profile. The consumer mix shift is very positive, and the premium-brand “hook” strategy should continue to capture more traffic. With the unit-growth strategy also proving to work, I see a clearer path for earnings to compound into FY26 and beyond.

data.title: ArcBest: Stay Bullish That ARCB Can Hit Its EPS Guide
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to ArcBest Corp. (ARCB) as I believed it can achieve its 2028 adj. EPS guidance given the fundamental drivers. I maintain my buy rating because the drivers behind the 2028 EPS target continue to be intact. The November tonnage rebound, steady pricing, and the growing contribution from Managed Solutions reinforce my view that ARCB can grow earnings even in a flat cycle.

November’s Volume Inflection

The main debate around ARCB’s equity story is the weak volume trends. When management gave the Q4 2025 guide, it was mentioned that October was still weak, with tonnage per day down 1.2% y/y, and that signals to the market that Q4 will see a similar weak volume trend. But with the November data out, I think it effectively resolves that concern. In November, ARCB saw a sharp reversal, with tonnage per day up 3% y/y and daily shipments up 3% y/y.

This gave me more confidence that ARCB can indeed grow earnings as I expected. Back in October, my view was that ARCB could hit the target even in a flat cycle, with the assumption that volumes will not recover meaningfully. But with the 3% tonnage growth seen in November, which, if it holds into 2026, adds another positive attribute into the growth equation. Remember that the ARCB business model has a lot of fixed costs embedded, which means even a small volume gain can translate into decent earnings upside.

The context of this volume growth is even better if we put it against the fact that manufacturing PMI contracted for the eighth straight month in October. This tells me that ARCB is likely taking share, which seems right given management explicitly said shipments per day increased ~3% sequentially from October to November (note this is not in line with historical seasonality).

Pricing Discipline Remains Intact

The one data point that some readers may take wrongly in the 8-K filing is that in November, billed revenue per hundredweight fell 2% y/y. At face value, that looks like pricing softness. But in reality, it is really a problem of mix. Because of the excess capacity in the truckload market, some of the LTL freight volume has been shifted to truckload, and that shift itself causes the weight profile inside the LTL network to change too. When that happens, yield per hundredweight naturally moves because of the mathematical mechanism.

The better metric to track for pricing is what customers actually agreed to on renewals. You have to look at the Q3 earnings to derive this. In Q3, ARCB secured average increases of 4.5% on contract renewals and deferred pricing, up from 4% in Q2. That tells us that shippers are willing to pay more (you can see that as the clearing price). If the LTL market were deteriorating, you wouldn’t be seeing sequential acceleration in contract pricing. Management also reiterated during the call that the broader pricing environment remains rational.

As the truckload capacity supply situation eases and heavier freight returns to LTL (i.e., the weight for both sides normalizes), yield metrics should recover as well. It is also worth noting that revenue per hundredweight excluding fuel declined 5% sequentially in November at the same time weight per shipment increased 5%. The way to understand this is that heavier freight dilutes yield per pound but adds density and efficiency to the network. So, you shouldn’t look at pricing and ignore the other metrics. I believe ARCB is optimizing for profit and network flow, and the pricing trend doesn’t capture it.

Managed Solutions

One part of the story that I think still isn’t getting enough credit is the integrated model, especially the Managed Solutions business. The bears would typically just lump this segment into the low-margin brokerage bucket, but I believe they are wrong, and the recent numbers prove this.

Before I get there, to understand why the Managed Solutions segment matters, you have to ignore the “brokerage” label. Traditional truckload brokerage is transactional in nature (it’s the same as you hailing a taxi for a one-off trip). In this managed solution model, ARCB effectively becomes the customer's logistics team. Instead of bidding on individual loads, ARCB integrates its technology directly into the customer's daily operations to manage their entire freight flow. In terms of revenue quality, I’d say this is way higher than the core business since the switching cost is extremely high (costly and disruptive for the customer to switch providers). And since ARCB is managing the whole process, the volume becomes recurring and predictable.

As for the number proof, the data supports this view. The fact that Asset-Light total daily shipment saw 3% growth in Q3 despite a soft freight market shows how sticky these relationships are. Unlike the core transactional freight that tends to go away when competitors compete on rates, this volume is contractual and recurring.

Valuation

My valuation methodology hasn’t changed. I still think the best way to value ARCB is to look at the midterm guide and attach a multiple to it. My core view is that the targeted $13.5 EPS is possible, and in fact, I think there is a strong potential for EPS to even touch the high end of the guide, $15. If that is the case, I believe the current 14x NTM PE is for sure too low. Like I have compared before, even TFI International (TFII) trades at 18x with a similar earnings growth profile as ARCB (similar >20%). Again, my math is simple. If ARCB trades at the same multiple, the shares are worth $270.

Investment Risk

With PMI contracting for eight straight months, if it drags on any further, it would ultimately weigh on ARCB's overall growth, and that could delay the timeline for ARCB to hit its guided targets. There are also execution risks. For instance, the recent service issues drove a decline in Mastio rankings, and a failure to reverse this could hurt ARCB market share.

Conclusion

I stay bullish on ARCB because volumes are still stabilizing, pricing stays rational, and Managed Solutions is showing its worth. I believe the stock is still cheap if ARCB can hit its guided EPS, and the early signs from November suggest the freight cycle may be turning sooner than expected. If ARCB can deliver as expected, I expect multiples to go up, closing the gap vs. peers.

data.title: The Hartford: An Insurer I Remain Bullish On, As Its Business Insurance Niche Grows
data.description:
Today's Pick: A Major P&C Insurer With A Recent Earnings Beat

This past summer, I showed just how easy it can be to understand the insurance business, by writing about The Hartford Insurance Group (HIG), which has gone up around +6% since my buy rating in July.

In fact, this stock is up nearly 16% since an even earlier coverage of mine, in Oct. 2024, when I was also bullish.

This summer in particular, I recognized certain macroeconomic demand forecasts favoring the liability and employee benefits niche, but also pointed out the risk of catastrophic weather events when it comes to insuring property.

Besides those niches, according to its Seeking Alpha profile, this firm also offers workers' comp, car insurance, and marine, among others.

My updated coverage today takes into view some elements of its last earnings results reported in late October, when it beat estimates, and also adds some additional angles since my last article.

My Rating: Buy Reaffirmed

My thesis today argues to keep my prior buy rating on this stock.

The firm is one of my favorite insurers on the watchlist since I've began writing for this platform, and it has shown through several upside factors why it continues to be a bullish case, including being a steady dividend grower with strong credit ratings and low leverage risk, but also enough future upside expected in the share price, positive EPS estimates, and proven organic growth in key segments like business insurance.

Below is my worksheet which helps visualize how I got to this score and what factors drove it.

Long-Term Return vs S&P500: Neck to Neck With The Index

Today's analysis tries to be as future-looking as possible, trying to guess what the music may do in a year and beyond when it comes to this stock, and has longer-term investors in mind, however some historic-looking data also plays a role in the story.

In this first section, let's get a sense of the larger picture by comparing this stock's performance vs a major index like the S&P500, and how a few comparable peers did vs the index. This is to gauge if this stock has already proven itself in price performance, vs an investor having invested instead in an S&P500 index fund.

In this comparison, using momentum data, we see that Hartford had a 10 year price performance of +208%, putting it very close to the index which saw +241%, and also ahead of two key property/casualty insurers, Chubb (CB) and AIG (AIG).

Some readers have suggested the S&P500 is skewed heavily by major tech stocks, and that is understandable and I acknowledge such concerns, but nevertheless it remains a major market benchmark and this shows Hartford has a long track record of achieving competitive price performance vs the benchmark and some key peers, so for this reason I gave it a low bullish score in this category.

Sector Risk: The AI Opportunity Is There, But So is Ongoing Risk of Catastrophe Events

Let's talk about some risks and opportunities in the property/casualty insurance sector.

Unlike primarily life insurers, who insure mortality events but also may offer annuities/retirement solutions, we should think about P&C as a niche focusing on insuring homes, businesses, cars, and so on.

This also means it can see spikes in claims during major wildfires, weather events, and such, impacting profits that quarter, and from a future-view the risk is there is no 100% certainty this far in advance as to when and where a major weather event or fire may occur, however there are past statistics on certain regions more or less prone to those.

Last January, for instance, the Insurance Journal reported that:

Preliminary data show insurance companies have paid out more than $4 billion for losses from the biggest two of the Los Angeles-area wildfires that swept through the region and destroyed tens of thousands of homes earlier this month.

A potential opportunity in this sector, however, remains the potential of AI to change the productivity dynamic. For instance, the Boston Consulting Group wrote this past September:

The industry stands at the threshold of an AI-driven transformation that promises to reshape underwriting, claims, distribution, and customer engagement.

So, as a follower of AI trends, I see some of as an upside driver, if it can improve cost-effectiveness of the above processes without sacrificing service and the human experience as well, rather than replacing the human element. After all, my understanding is that insurance agents and reps have to be licensed in each US state before selling insurance, so there is still a barrier to entry as it is a highly regulated industry, also an upside as it limits the playing field. You can't just launch a startup in your garage and call yourself a licensed insurance agent, if you're not licensed.

Also, I want to point out signs of resilience in this industry, and resilience in the face of various market headwinds is one feature to look for in companies poised for future upside and stability.

Consider what industry group NAIC.org wrote earlier this year:

Despite heavy catastrophe losses, including the costliest wildfires on record, the U.S. Property & Casualty (P&C) industry recorded its best mid-year underwriting gain in nearly 20 years.

So, in an industry known for the need for prudent risk management, I think the opportunities for upside in this space are there, but of course some caution is needed due to the risk of catastrophe losses, hence I am in an upper neutral range with my score for this category.

Company Growth: Momentum in the Business Insurance Segment is Evident

In this section, let's talk about company-specific growth opportunities and risks, and we will use some points from Q3 earnings results to tell the story briefly.

First of all, the Hartford brand actually is more than just insurance, as according to its Q3 investor deck (pg. 3) it has a diversified revenue model that also includes an employee benefits line, and mutual funds (Hartford Funds).

I think this reduces future downside risk of over-reliance on a single product line, such as simply selling home insurance, and then bearing the brunt of wildfire or wind losses in a particular region.

On that note, we can see how this plays out in YoY earnings growth, as one business segment saw a slight YoY decline (employee benefits) but the other segments saw growth, with business insurance seeing +35% YoY core earnings growth, according to the same investor deck (pg 5):

When it comes to growth opportunity that drives future upside, in this sector I look for both organic growth (new insurance policies sold) as that can drive future premiums income, but also new business acquisitions, growth in premiums, and perhaps new moneys coming into the asset management segment like Hartford Funds.

The investor deck (pg 7) mentions within business insurance they saw growth in written premiums, "driven in part by strong new business growth."

That certainly is a sign of organic growth in one of their core business segments, business insurance.

In their mutual fund segment, the deck (pg 10) shows that although there are still net outflows to the firm's mutual funds/ETFs, there has been a considerable YoY improvement, with the outflows cut down to $25MM, so a major improvement trend starting, but more is needed to show positive net inflows over a longer period:

The mutual fund business is important as it is fees driven, so new net inflows of monies can contribute to future fees income, so the firm is not only reliant on insurance premiums or investment income.

When comparing against 4 peers in the P&C niche, Seeking Alpha data shows a positive upside factor, as Hartford is expected to have +6.9% fwd revenue growth, with only Allstate (ALL) beating it in this peer group:

So, with still some improvement to be made in the funds business, the otherwise notable momentum driver is the business insurance niche, so for the reasons above I gave this category a low bullish score.

Profit Margins: Competitive Positioning Among Large P&C Insurers, While AI Could Add Value

Here, let's consider some points about profit margins.

Since I love comparing to peers, the below table I pulled shows that Hartford is quite competitive to key peers in the P&C niche, and the two metrics I would highlight is the EBITDA and net income margins:

You'll notice something different than in all my recent coverage of REITs, and that is there is not such a wide gap between EBITDA and net margins like with many REITs, who I mentioned often use depreciation on property to bring down reported profits.

In the example above, the gap is not so pronounced, and this firm has exceeded 12% margins in both, but a key peer like Chubb is still well ahead, indicating to me that they may be somewhat more efficient at turning a profit that Hartford is.

Hartford is not a major outlier in this group, though, to warrant concern.

Still, as mentioned earlier, this business is prone to swings in catastrophe events impacting earnings. Its Q3 press release (pg 1) mentioned, for example:

P&C current accident year -CAY catastrophe - CAT losses in third quarter 2025 of $70 million, before tax, compared with $247 million, before tax in third quarter 2024.

Hence, you can see the wide variance that can occur in CAT losses from one year to next, and this can impact earnings of course.

Thus, given the impressive margins of other peers like Chubb, and the potential for swings in catastrophe losses which can vary greatly, there is cause to be neutral in this category, but I would also mention again the role of AI in improving cost effectiveness.

According to an article by company, Dustin Kingsbury, vice president of Employee Benefit Claims at The Hartford, said:

AI enables us to remove administrative burden from the equation to allow our claims team more time to connect with claimants along their journey. While AI can scan and evaluate hundreds of data points in seconds, it takes human wisdom to contextualize the intelligence and make decisions.

So, because the AI trend is continuing, and who knows what further potential it can bring, particularly to this industry, that adds some points to the upside, since I think mega insurers will be under pressure to keep bureaucratic costs/processes at bay.

With that said, I am giving this category a low bullish score, but I also recognize that its peers may also be jumping on the AI bandwagon too.

Dividend Growth & Coverage: A Proven Growth Engine With Low Payout Ratio

Here, let's consider the company's dividend growth and coverage.

Because dividend yield is under 2%, and yield can change as the price changes, I would not focus so much on yield if I were investing in this stock but on growth.

In that regard, this stock has exceeded its sector average by achieved a 10 year dividend growth of +10.72%.

Visually, we can see how that looks in the graph below:

While this may be historic data, it also shows a positive upward trend that is steady and was not impacted by major macro events like the global pandemic or spikes in inflation and interest rates in recent years. You may remember the saying from grade school that slow and steady wins the race, and it seems that could be applied to insurance in some ways. Though it is not the cool new Silicon Valley startup hyped up in major media, it has proven it can return dividends to shareholders and also grow them.

By contrast, some hotel REITs I've covered have seen their dividends cut as that sector was heavily impacted by the year of travel disruptions.

In a more future-view, a key metric to gauge ability to sustain and cover this dividend is the payout ratio. My benchmark is usually 75% or less, and in this case the payout ratio for HIG is 16.8%, so that seems to leave lots of wiggle room to lessen the risk of a dividend cut if things go downhill with cashflow.

Of course, I welcome reader comments on what you personally think is a good or bad payout ratio, since this can sometimes be subjective.

Otherwise, I think the evidence adds to the bullish score for this stock.

Balance Sheet Risk: An Investment-Grade Stock, But Interest Rate Risk Exists

When it comes to balance sheet risk, I picked three elements to present: the D/E ratio, the asset risk exposure in their investment holdings, and their credit ratings.

When it comes to comparing the D/E to peers, we see that this entire peer group is relatively low, and with Hartford at just 0.23, indicating a very low leverage risk in relation to equity:

For more risk-averse investors, or who want to add a lower-risk (though not risk free) stock to a mixed portfolio, I think this one is worth adding to a watch list at least and then researching further, and more due diligence on the investors part.

Particularly, since this is a regulated industry, I believe certain risk levers have to be monitored, to ensure an insurer is well capitalized. So, I don't expect any extreme cases of this firm investing in some exotic MBS portfolio in the flavor of some firms during 2007/2008 Wall Street.

Instead, according to the investor deck (pg 12), we see that securitized products and mortgage loans are a somewhat lesser part of asset exposure at this firm, while the largest exposure is to corporate fixed income (36% of the portfolio).

While this scenario provides for interest income from such assets, and also liquidity if those are securities that are tradeable, it also can mean the bonds may sometimes have to be sold at a loss to raise needed cash or to re-invest in higher-yielding bonds.

A large exposure to fixed income, and not much equity, also can create interest rate risk, and since interest rates can be impacted by Fed decisions (outside the control of the company and its management) that is a risk for investors to consider. As you probably know, the inverse relationship exists between bond values and interest rates.

To add some third-party perspective, I was curious what major rating agencies said about this firm.

In this case, not one but three major agencies gave this firm's senior and junior debt an investment-grade rating, according to the company website:

So, I think from a future view these strong credit ratings and low leverage risk position the firm for more favorable financing terms in the future, than a firm with negative ratings and already high leverage. More favorable credit terms could mean lower interest expense in the future, which is an upside in my opinion.

Given the low D/E and strong credit ratings, as well as lesser exposure to securitized products and mortgages, but also considering interest rate risk, I though a low bullish score would be fair for this category.

Chart Technical Indicators: Signs Point To A Hold

In this section, I'll briefly show a technical chart indicator and what I think it could mean for this stock.

In this case, I compared the 50-day SMA vs the 200-day SMA, over the course of a year. There were no golden crosses (buy signals / 50 day crossing above the 200 day) and no death crosses (sell signal / 50 day crossing below the 200-day).

With the two moving averages running relatively parallel for a while now, I think the most fair call to make is to call this a hold, if looking based solely on the chart above, with no clear buy or sell indicators.

I welcome reader comments on your view of the technicals in this chart, and do you agree it indicates holding on, and why or why not.

Valuation vs Future Upside: Signs Point To Enough Upside Along With Reasonable Valuation

In this final topic, I will briefly consider valuation metrics and also future price targets.

Here is my valuation worksheet I made:

In this comparison, using peer data from Seeking Alpha, we can see that HIG is slightly undervalued to the peer group when it comes to fwd P/E, a forward-looking metric, but is somewhat overvalued to the group in terms of the trailing price/book value.

The question often arises is whether I think it is a justified valuation, and why. In this case, the consensus EPS estimates have had 17 upward revisions, and expect +22% YoY EPS growth this year, followed by 5-8% in the next few years, so it seems HIG could actually be undervalued. However, it is also trading at a premium to book value too. So, it seems to be more in the "reasonable" valuation range.

With that said, let's see how that fwd P/E, and future EPS estimate, plays a role in my future price target worksheet:

In this example, my own price target is close to that of the avg Wall St consensus, which does not always happen, so the implied upside for this stock is between 7 - 9%. I think that justifies at least a hold, if not a cautious bullish sentiment. I have this category a high neutral rating, and it helped boost the stock's overall analysis which continues to support a bullish case.

Challenge to My Rating: What Will Commercial Mortgages Do in 2026?

A potential challenge to my bullish optimism, beyond what I already talked about in this article or included in my rating analysis, could come from exposure to mortgage loans.

Although having such assets in its investment portfolio is not in and of itself a red flag for concern, especially since it is part of a diversified mix of assets, as I showed earlier, I think the risk to a bullish outlook could come if there is a widespread series of commercial mortgage defaults.

Whether or not this firm's mortgage loans become impaired may not even matter, since investor sentiment can react to quickly moving headlines and fear metrics driven by other insurers or banks, so any exposure to mortgage loans by a company that has to meet strict capitalization requirements as an insurer, could put downward pressure on such a stock.

Conclusion: Agree with Bullish Consensus

The consensus today is bullish across the board, and so am I this time.

In conclusion, I've shown today several reasons why the upside potential on this stock could very well outweigh the downside risks, with those downside risks primarily coming from the nature of P&C insurance and being prone to catastrophe events causing claims to be paid out on a large scale.

However, it is not the firm's first rodeo as it has shown it can be resilient over many years and impacted by several such claim events, yet continues to post impressive results within its peer group.

So, my call to action is to continue considering this one for your watch list, and include it in dividend ideas for the month.

data.title: My Most Contrarian Investment For 2026: Caesars Entertainment
data.description:
Right now, there are growing fears that we might be headed into a recession.

The job market is cooling at a rapid pace.

Consumer spending is softening.

And the trade war is leading to higher input costs and significant uncertainty.

The market is reacting to this by running away from cyclical stocks, which have taken a beating so far this year.

Even worse are businesses that also rely on international tourism, as many have decided to boycott the USA and spend their money elsewhere.

A great example of that is Caesars Entertainment, Inc. (CZR), which is one of the leading casino operators in the US:

The market fears that as the economy weakens further, people will spend less on gambling and entertainment, which are some of the purest forms of discretionary spending. Moreover, a lot of international tourists, especially from Canada, Mexico, Europe, and China, are now skipping their trips to Las Vegas.

The impact has been so significant that the Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority is now taking actions to try to lure back Canadians. Here is what they said in a recent statement:

"A portion of our friends in Canada are not happy with us right now. We want them to come back, but we understand they may not be ready to do that." Steve Hill, CEO of the Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority

This led to a soft summer for Las Vegas and caused CZR's stock to drop another 30% this year:

That's after already dropping significantly prior to that. In total, it is now down 80% from its 2021 highs. Talk about a contrarian investment!

All else held equal, we agree that a weaker economy and lower international tourism would justify a lower valuation for a company like CZR as it will inevitably lead to lower profits at its Las Vegas casinos.

But all else is not equal.

Most importantly, the company's digital business is exploding to the upside at the moment. In just two years, its revenue has grown by 139%, and its management now expects its digital business to reach an annual run rate of $500+ million of adjusted EBITDA by Q4 2026.

We think that CZR is in an ideal position to compete in this space because it has the biggest loyalty program in the casino sector, with over 65 million people, and it can leverage its casinos to grow its digital business with limited marketing spend. As an example, as people spend money at the Caesars Palace, they then gain Caesars Reward Points, which they can then use to gamble on the app. Similarly, as they gamble through the app, they get Rewards Points, which they can then redeem for free drinks, meals, and hotel rooms at CZR's casinos. Its competitors, who run a digital-only business, cannot offer such perks as cost-efficiently.

They are now scaling the digital platform at a rapid pace, and as it hits $500+ million of annual EBITDA in 2026, it will be worth a lot of money.

According to Texas Capital analyst David Bain, the average EBITDA multiple of pure-play digital peers DraftKings Inc. (DKNG), Flutter Entertainment plc (FLUT), and Rush Street Interactive, Inc. (RSI) is 19.2x.

If we applied that multiple to the $500 million of annual EBITDA, CZR's digital business would be worth $9.6 billion.

That's huge when you consider that the company's current market is just above $4.7 billion.

And that's just the start. The management has previously noted that they expect the rapid growth to continue beyond 2026 as they expand into new jurisdictions and make further improvements to their platform. Here is what the CEO said on the recent conference call:

And as you look forward and think about '26, '27, '28, where you may have new iGaming jurisdictions, where I would expect our share of a new jurisdiction given our product and the momentum in that business would probably be something like 2x what our share is in the legacy markets. You can start to talk yourself into some pretty bullish outcomes in Digital, and we see no indication that anything is slowing down. For us, the rollout of a single wallet in Nevada is a wonderful customer acquisition tool.

If you recall before that, all the customers that would come to our properties in Nevada and open a Caesars Sports account, so they could bet while they were in Vegas would go home and have to open a separate account, which is obviously less than ideal. So we think from a customer acquisition standpoint outside of Nevada, that's going to be a powerful tool and add to the momentum that we've got going in this space. So I don't want to -- I've taken so much grief over the $500 million target that we're right on the precipice of, I'm hesitant to immediately put another target out there. But I'd say we're going to generate substantially more than $500 million of EBITDA from Digital, if you're looking out a few years here. [emphasis added]

So in just a few years from now, CZR's digital business alone could be worth as much as its entire enterprise value today.

Given the momentum and the significant competitive advantages, I think that CZR's digital business deserves to trade at a multiple at least in line with its peers. But even if you applied a considerably lower multiple, there is significant value here, and it is still overlooked today because they just had their first big quarters, and all the focus has been on the soft Summer in Las Vegas.

You may recall that Carl Icahn took a position in the stock last year at a materially higher level, and it is likely because he predicted this growth in the digital business. Now, this business is at an inflection point, and yet, the stock is much cheaper. CZR's management agrees that the stock price doesn't reflect the momentum of their digital business and has been buying back shares.

Beyond that, CZR should significantly benefit from lower interest rates.

It has quite a bit of leverage, and it owns a lot of real estate, which it is expected to monetize via sale and leaseback transactions with companies like VICI Properties (VICI) and Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI) over the coming years. Lower interest rates should increase transaction activity and lead to lower cap rates, improving the conditions for CZR to sell assets. We have previously estimated that its real estate alone could be worth nearly its entire enterprise value, which means that it could sell assets to pay off debt and buy back shares to create a lot of value.

Finally, while Vegas had a softer Summer, it is not as if it was a ghost town either. Occupancy rates are still in the 90s, and gaming revenue actually grew in July despite the lower traffic. Some softness is expected in the near term, especially if the economy weakens, but over the long run, we expect Vegas to do just fine.

Ultimately, the stock is very cheap based on near-term temporary concerns, but the company has many catalysts to create and unlock value over the long run.

They could sell a chunk of their real estate and/or spin off their digital business to return capital to shareholders.

My fair value estimate is about 3x higher than the current share price, and it could surge even higher than that over the next few years if they continue to successfully scale up the digital business.

Remember that the stock traded at $120 just a few years ago when casino stocks were hot, interest rates were low, and the market wasn't concerned about a recession. I think that it was overpriced back then, but it is also underpriced today at just $22. Fair value is in the middle ground, and we could get back there already in the coming years as the focus shifts to the rapid growth of the digital business and real estate asset monetization.

For all these reasons, we own a position at High Yield Landlord as part of our Core Portfolio, which aims to maximize total returns.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why I Just Loaded Up On Aris Mining Ahead Of 2026
data.description:
With the recent flurry of news, it’s a good time to reassess the risk-reward profile of Aris Mining (ARMN). In this update, I’ll start by comparing Aris with 22 gold-mining peers to identify the key drivers behind their sustained market outperformance or underperformance—in other words, why some become industry leaders while others fall behind. I’ll then circle back and evaluate Aris in light of those drivers, with the goal of gaining a deeper understanding of this emerging gold producer in Latin America.

Here’s how I separate the winning gold stocks from the dogs

The group

I selected 22 gold producers as Aris Mining’s comparison group, including three Australia-focused miners and four companies that operate exclusively in Africa. I had to limit the number of companies in the study, which unfortunately means leaving out some solid names. However, this selection bias shouldn’t prevent the key trends from emerging.

  • The Australian sub-group is: Gold Road Resources (ELKMF), Northern Star Resources (NESRF), and Westgold (WGXRF).
  • The African sub-group is: Caledonia Mining (CMCL), Pan African Resources (PAFRF), Perseus Mining (PMNXF), and Resolute Mining (RMGGF).
  • The remainder of the comparison group includes: Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), Alamos Gold (AGI), Barrick Gold (B), Centerra Gold (CGAU), Eldorado Gold (EGO), Equinox Gold (EQX), G Mining Ventures (GMINF), Hochschild Mining (HCHDF), IAMGOLD (IAG), Kinross Gold (KGC), Lundin Gold (LUGDF), Newmont (NEM), SSR Mining (SSRM).

I decided to use EV per in-situ gold ounce as the primary valuation metric. Statistically, about 69.3% of a gold producer’s enterprise value can be explained by its in-situ gold-equivalent resources, as indicated by the R² of 0.693. That suggests a strong relationship, leaving 30.7% unexplained, which can be attributable to other factors such as resource quality (average grade, orebody continuity, open-pittability, metallurgy), cost structure (including infrastructure access) and margins, development stage, and above-ground risks (fiscal terms, currency, permitting, jurisdiction), as illustrated in Figure 1. It is worth noting that the EV/Production metric actually has a weak positive linear relationship with EV, as evidenced by an R² of 0.168.

Ounces Are Not Equal: The hidden variables behind headline in-situ resource numbers

Like any resource investment, gold miners compete on costs, typically measured by all-in sustaining cost (AISC). AISC represents the total cost to sustain current mining operations, including direct production costs, sustaining capital expenditures, exploration expenses, and corporate G&A. AISC tends to rise naturally as grades decline and mining costs increase over time—especially under less-frugal operators.

There are countless ways to end up with a high AISC. However, there are really only two reliable ways to drive it down: either by mining an exceptionally continuous, high-grade orebody, as at Lundin Gold’s Fruta del Norte mine, or by mining open pits followed by heap-leach operations, as illustrated in Figure 2. The former is straightforward: when your average grade is five times the industry average, you only need to mine and process one-fifth of the ore to produce the same ounces of gold—and have one-fifth of the tailings to store. The latter—exemplified by G Mining (Tocantinzinho, Oko West), B2Gold (Goose, Back River, Gramalote, Fekola, Otjikoto, Masbate), and Perseus (Edikan, Sissingué, Yaouré, Meyas Sand, Nyanzaga)—deserves more investor attention. It turns out you can still achieve industry-leading costs even when your mines have average grades, as long as you mine at surface and process oxide ore through heap leaching at exceptionally low cost. Regarding cost, especially energy intensity, the variance between open-pit and underground mining is debated in the literature. Some studies find open-pit mining to be significantly less costly, while others suggest the opposite. These differing views may stem from sampling differences. Ultimately, cost depends on the specifics of each mine—for example, the strip ratio for open pits and the orebody depth and grades for underground mines. That said, our data indicate that the lowest AISC values are found both in open-pit operations and in extremely high-grade underground mines, such as Fruta del Norte.

By the way, I touched on this strategy in a previous article. Minera Alamos (MAIFF) attempted to follow the open-pit, heap-leach model in Mexico. Although its progress was slowed by permitting under the Sheinbaum administration, the strategy itself remains valid.

The standouts: The Hidden Drivers of Gold Stock Valuations

Of these 23 gold miners, three clearly stand out as especially favored by the market: Agnico Eagle, Alamos Gold, and Lundin Gold, as shown in figures 3 and 4.

Lundin Gold represents a miner’s dream. Its Fruta del Norte mine in Ecuador hosts one of the highest concentrations of gold within a compact volume measuring 1.3 km long, 60–160 m wide, and 300 m subvertical, as evidenced in Figure 5. FDN boasts high grades (averaging 6.6 g/t Au—five times the industry average, as shown in Figure 2), low costs (AISC of $1,036/oz Au), high margins (EBITDA margin >70% as of Q3 2025), and low subsurface risk (exceptional orebody continuity) as well as low above-ground risk (the host province of Zamora-Chinchipe is mining-friendly).

Alamos Gold is favored by the market for its track record of rapid production growth while lowering risk by focusing on safe jurisdictions—primarily Canada, where 89% of its NAV is located—and divesting assets in high-risk regions. Over the longer term, it expects to grow production to 900 kozpa from the 2025 mid-point guidance of 570 koz, all self-funded. By 2027, it believes economies of scale will drive its AISC down to $1,175/oz from the 2025 guidance of $1,425/oz, further expanding margins.

Agnico Eagle also focuses on safe jurisdictions—Canada, Australia, Mexico, and Finland—but it has achieved another level of diversified scale that provides highly visible long-term growth. Agnico is known for flawless execution of its growth plans and relentless cost control, almost in the style of Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ).

Under the previously discussed investment principle, companies like Agnico, Alamos Gold, and Lundin Gold are rewarded with premium valuation multiples because they can be counted on to reduce costs, maintain or even expand margins, and deliver profitable growth. These factors come together to drive value creation, which can be expressed as follows:

  • EV = [EV/EBITDA] × (Gold price − AISC) × Production × (1 + CAGR)ᵀⁱᵐᵉ
  • EV = [EV/In-situ resources] × In-situ resources = [EV/In-situ resources] × Resource life × Production × (1 + CAGR)ᵀⁱᵐᵉ

where the average resource life is around 50 years, as shown in Figure 6. Up to a certain point (more on this later), scaling up production reduces costs through economies of scale. Lower costs mean higher margins and greater resilience through the cycle.

These three market darlings share several other common traits. They all have squeaky-clean balance sheets: as of the end of Q3 2025, Lundin Gold had no debt and $494 million in cash; Alamos Gold had $213 million in net cash; and Agnico Eagle had $2.16 billion in net cash. Thanks to its aggressive debt reduction, Moody’s upgraded Agnico Eagle’s credit rating to A3 from Baa1. They also use their free cash flow to reward shareholders. Lundin Gold has been raising its annualized dividend at a 58.9% CAGR since initiating payouts in 2022; it currently offers a 4.2% yield. Alamos has paid dividends since 2010 and is now considering buybacks with $600 million of cash on its balance sheet. Agnico Eagle has paid dividends every year since 1983, currently yielding 1.0%, and has increased its quarterly dividend fivefold since the last gold bear-market bottom in 2015. In the first nine months of 2025, it repurchased 2.33 million common shares for $300 million.

How to pull away from the pack in 2025 and beyond

Size plays an important role in helping gold miners benefit from economies of scale, as we discussed above; however, bigger is not always better. Once a company reaches a certain level of in-situ gold-equivalent ounces—somewhere around 150–200 Moz AuEq, as shown in Figure 7—the market seems to stop rewarding the accumulation of additional underground resources. That is logical from several angles. Financially, holding millions of ounces you lack the capital or manpower to develop is a poor use of exploration or acquisition dollars. It is usually far more efficient to divest those non-core ounces to someone who can put them into production, and to use part of the proceeds to reduce interest-bearing debt—essentially following the path Alamos Gold has taken. Geologically, a company the size of Newmont can only chase very large deposits, but few giant primary gold deposits remain for a pure-play gold miner to grow. The great gold giants historically came from a limited set of deposit styles—mesothermal, Carlin-type, porphyry/epithermal, intrusion-related, Witwatersrand, VMS, and IOCG—but many of these are now largely exhausted and major new discoveries are rare. Porphyries, such as Filo del Sol, add another issue: own too many, and you drift toward being a copper producer, forfeiting the valuation premium of gold-focused miners. Lastly, from an organizational standpoint, taking on too many projects leads to corporate bloat—large headcounts, slow decision-making, and weak capital allocation.

I believe that over the next few years, as gold continues to move higher, we may see the emergence of a group of large-cap gold producers valued between $30 billion and $70 billion, as shown in Figure 8. This could happen through consolidation among the numerous junior and intermediate producers, or through a “whale-fall” effect—divestments of non-core mines or undeveloped resources that Newmont and Barrick have neither the capacity nor the intent to advance. It is an exciting time for growth-oriented companies like Aris Mining.

Now that we’ve pinpointed the key drivers of value creation in gold mining, let’s apply them to a deep look at Aris Mining. We’ll focus on four things: (1) whether Aris has a visible, achievable production-growth path as gold continues to move higher; (2) how the company intends to drive costs lower; (3) whether management is allocating capital and handling corporate finance prudently—especially when it comes to reducing debt; and (4) what concrete steps they’re taking to reward shareholders.

Production growth

Asset portfolio

In February 2020, Gran Colombia Gold Corp. spun off its Marmato Mine into Caldas Gold Corp., retaining a 71.8% ownership stake in Caldas Gold, ostensibly to accelerate the development of Marmato Lower (Figure 9). However, under then-CEO Serafino Iacono, Caldas went on to acquire South American Resources for its Juby project in Ontario.

By 2020, Canadian Business Hall of Famer Ian Telfer had overseen the $10 billion merger of Goldcorp with Newmont and retired as its chairman, while Neil Woodyer had just sold Leagold to Equinox Gold for $578 million. For readers unfamiliar with influential figures like Telfer and Woodyer in the Canadian mining sector, Telfer co-founded TVX Gold Inc. in 1983, growing it into a global mid-tier gold producer before merging it into Kinross Gold (KGC) in 1998. Together with Frank Giustra, he acquired Wheaton River Minerals Ltd. and, through a series of acquisitions, transformed it from a C$17 million shell into a company valued at $2.4 billion. In 2004 and 2005, they spun out Wheaton River’s silver-streaming business into Silver Wheaton Corp., which later became Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), and merged Wheaton River with Goldcorp, where Telfer served as CEO (2005–2006) and Chairman (2006–2019). Woodyer founded Endeavour Financial Corp. in 1988 and beginning in 2009, together with Frank Giustra, transitioned it into Endeavour Mining (EDVMF). Through a series of acquisitions and new builds in West Africa, they developed it into a 500,000-oz-per-year gold producer operating four mines. In 2016, Woodyer stepped down as CEO of Endeavour Mining and founded the Mexico- and Brazil-focused Leagold Mining Corp., which was merged into Equinox Gold Corp. (EQX) four years later.

In November 2020, Telfer assembled an all-star team with Woodyer as CEO to take over Caldas and rename it Aris Gold, while concurrently raising $85 million, including $38 million from the incoming team, to advance the Marmato expansion project. In March 2022, Aris secured operatorship of the Soto Norte project in Colombia as the first step in building a portfolio of projects to support aggressive organic growth, as illustrated in Figure 10.

While the Telfer–Woodyer team laid the foundation for Aris Gold’s next decade, Gran Colombia Gold—later known as GCM Mining—went on a shopping spree, expanding hastily outside Colombia into Spain, Canada, and Guyana in search of asset diversification, and in the process squandering cash flows from the high-grade Segovia Mine. In the end, what it really needed was a serially successful leadership team like Telfer–Woodyer. Thus, in September 2022, GCM and Aris Gold recombined to form Aris Mining.

As a result of the GCM–Aris merger, Aris Mining inherited a portfolio of four core assets, as shown in Figure 11: the high-grade underground Segovia Mine; the underground Marmato Upper Mine with a bulk-mineable root; the high-grade Soto Norte deposit for underground mining; and the open-pittable Toroparu deposit in the Cuyuní–Mazaruni greenstone belt of the Guiana Shield, which forms part of the same contiguous geological province as the West African Shield (before the two were separated by the opening of the Central Atlantic)—a geology the Aris team knows extremely well from their Endeavour years. Together, these four assets give Aris a total of 32.05 Moz of measured, indicated, and inferred contained gold resources to support its organic growth objectives (Fig. 9).

Production growth outlook

At Segovia, Aris Mining completed and commissioned a second mill in June 2025, increasing processing capacity by 50% to 3,000 tpd. The company is currently ramping up operations toward 300,000 ozpa. At Marmato Lower, first gold is expected in H2 2026, and once ramp-up is complete, the mine is expected to produce 200,000 ozpa. I expect Aris to reach its 500,000 ozpa run-rate target by 2027.

Aris Mining plans to submit the formal environmental license application for Soto Norte to the Colombian environmental authority (ANLA) in H1 2026, which will initiate the regulatory review process. In Colombia, this typically includes technical evaluation, public participation, clarifications, and potential requests for additional information. Assuming the process takes roughly 24 months, as is often the case, permits may be obtained by mid-2028, with Soto Norte potentially beginning production by 2030 and adding 263,000 ozpa. That means Toroparu may pour first gold ahead of Soto Norte.

The Toroparu prefeasibility study is expected by October 2026. Aris Mining may reach FID by next November, although the company has already begun exploring ways to accelerate construction. Financing Toroparu will not be an issue, as the company is capable of self-funding the US$800 million build. I expect first gold in 2029, adding 235,000 ozpa upon ramp-up. This would put the company at roughly 1 Mozpa by then, implying a 21% CAGR in gold production over the next seven years, as illustrated in Figure 12.

Cost control

A gold miner’s AISC is determined by mining, processing, overhead, sustaining capex, exploration, and accrued reclamation costs. However, the gold price has a major impact: royalties (about 4% of revenue), local taxes (0.4–1%), community agreements, Contract Mining Partners (or CMP) agreements, and contractor rates all rise when gold moves higher in Colombia. In general, every $1,000/oz increase in the gold price can add roughly $120–$250/oz to AISC. In addition, the Colombian peso has weakened about 38% against the U.S. dollar since 2019, which has inflated USD-denominated costs such as equipment and chemicals, partly offset by local labor costs.

The roughly 85% increase in the gold price over the past five years would have pushed the Segovia Operations’ AISC to about $1,740/oz, based on historical sensitivity (Figure 13). In reality, Aris Mining kept AISC near $1,560/oz over the past two years, allowing only about a $460/oz rise from the 2019–2022 levels (Figure 14).

I actually believe Aris is well positioned to reduce Segovia’s AISC in the near future. CMP ore typically has lower head grade than ore from Aris’ own operations, which is partly why CMP-related AISC tends to be higher (Figure 15a & b). As recently as Q2 2024, CMPs accounted for about 53% of gold-sales volume, so their higher AISC has been the main reason Segovia’s overall AISC sits where it does. Since then, Aris-owned operations have steadily increased their share of total gold sold, setting the stage for Segovia’s AISC to converge toward the AISC reported from Aris’ own mining (Figure 15c).

In addition, with the second mill successfully commissioned in June 2025—boosting total capacity by 50% to 3,000 tpd (Figure 14)—Segovia has delivered a meaningful increase in throughput and production. Segovia milled an average of 2,553 tpd in Q3 2025, up 29.2% sequentially, and still 17.5% below nameplate capacity, while maintaining head grades near 10 g/t Au and recoveries around 96%. Gold production at Segovia rose 12.5% from Q2 to Q3 2025 (Figure 15d).

At the Marmato gold mine, Aris Mining is currently producing only from the upper, narrow-vein zone, using a mill designed for 1,250 tpd but realistically operating at a capacity of 1,000 tpd (Figure 16). Production from Marmato Lower—the bulk-mining zone—won’t begin until H2 2026, when the new 5,000 tpd mill starts up, eventually ramping the operation to more than 200,000 oz of gold per year. Marmato Upper appears to break even at roughly $2,654/oz. When gold rose above that level in 2025, Aris raised mill throughput by 19%, and we began to see meaningful production growth. Higher output will help reduce costs at Marmato Upper, but its impact on corporate AISC will be limited because Marmato Upper accounts for only about 10% of total gold production. The real driver of lower Marmato AISC will be the startup of Marmato Lower. According to the 2022 pre-feasibility study based on a $1,600/oz gold assumption, the expanded Marmato project carries an AISC of $1,003/oz. A simple regression of historical AISC against realized gold prices suggests that for every $1,000/oz increase in gold, Marmato AISC rises about $285/oz. At the Q3 2025 realized price of $3,494/oz, that implies an AISC of roughly $1,543/oz—almost $1,400/oz lower than the reported Q3 2025 AISC. Therefore, the startup of Marmato Lower is expected to lead to a major reduction in mine-site AISC.

My back-of-the-envelope estimate shows that once total production reaches 500,000 oz per year, Aris’ corporate AISC would be under $1,600/oz if gold remains at the Q3 2025 level, and no more than about $2,170/oz at the spot gold price of $4,150/oz. On top of that, the rise in annual production from the current 292,000-oz run rate to 500,000 oz will greatly dilute corporate fixed costs; and the CMP know-how gained at Segovia can be applied at Marmato, creating a steep learning curve there and meaningful cost savings. All else equal, I’m confident Aris will be far more efficient in the foreseeable future than it is today.

The upside

At The Natural Resources Hub, we don't build our investment thesis solely on rising gold prices; we therefore focus on factors the company, as a mine operator, controls. Besides gold bullishness and margin expansion from cost cutting, Aris Mining stock's upside will primarily be determined by two drivers: rerating and production growth.

Rerating

Aris Mining is currently valued at $111/oz of in-situ gold-equivalent resources, implying a 2.4× rerating potential to the “ex-Agnico, ex-Lundin Gold, and ex-Alamos” peer-group average of $260/oz AuEq, or a 5× rerating potential to the broader group average of $540/oz AuEq, as illustrated in Figure 17. Although I noted earlier that the EV/Production metric may be less indicative when comparing gold miners, Aris’s valuation of $8,193/ozpa still suggests a 2× rerating potential relative to the group average of $16,447/ozpa, as shown in Figure 18.

In terms of P/NAV, Aris Mining currently trades at 0.58× its net asset value based on technical reports that use gold-price assumptions ranging from $1,600 to $3,000/oz. I estimate that its P/NAV would decline to 0.23× under a $4,150/oz gold scenario. In the gold-mining industry, intermediate producers typically trade at 0.8–1.2× NAV, while senior producers trade at 1.0–1.5× NAV. It is therefore fair to conclude that Aris is significantly undervalued. Accordingly, I believe there is substantial hidden rerating potential in Aris Mining, ranging from 2× to as high as 5×.

Production growth

Aris Mining is executing a long-term plan to become a 1 Mozpa gold producer in Latin America. As discussed earlier (Figure 12), the company is on track to deliver 2× production growth from its 2024 base by 2027, following the start of commercial production at Marmato Lower, and more than 4× production growth from the 2024 base by 2031 by bringing Toroparu and Soto Norte on stream. The combination of a bullish gold price environment and flat or lower AISC should significantly amplify this production growth into industry-leading profit expansion. Between rerating and organic production (and profit) growth, I expect Aris Mining to deliver approximately (2–5) × 4 = 8–20× returns over the next 6–7 years.

The Aris Mining team has a track record of acquisitive growth. While this analysis focuses on organic growth, we should not rule out the possibility that the company pursues M&A to accelerate its expansion. For example, it would be logical for Aris Mining to consider acquiring Collective Mining (CNL), particularly its Apollo discovery, which is located immediately adjacent to Marmato (Figure 19)—though Collective Mining’s executive chairman, Ari Sussman, may not sell it cheaply. It would make perfect sense to unitize the Apollo discovery at Guayabales with Marmato to create a 20+ Moz gold district, over which Aris Mining could apply its CMP expertise at scale. The unitized Marmato–Guayabales district could serve as a cornerstone asset if Aris Mining intends to grow into a senior gold producer—much like Nevada Gold Mines for Newmont and Barrick, Canadian Malartic for Agnico Eagle Mines, Olimpiada for Polyus, or Geita for AngloGold Ashanti (AU). Additionally, Sussman—a serially successful mine builder with deep experience in Colombia—fits naturally into Aris Mining’s long-term executive succession planning and stands out as a strong potential successor to Woodyer when the time comes.

Catalysts

There are several near- and medium-term catalysts that can drive a rerating in Aris Mining and enable its production growth.

  • The acquisition of the remaining 49% interest in Soto Norte from Mubadala is expected to close by early December 2025. While the market has mostly shrugged at the deal because of perceived permitting uncertainty, it is highly impactful—not only because it adds 25% to proven and probable reserves and 17% to measured, indicated, and inferred resources (Figure 9), but also because these in-situ ounces were acquired at exceptionally low cost (see below).
  • At Segovia, the plant is expected to reach full capacity in Q4 2025, pushing production close to 300,000 ozpa, a 14% increase over Q3 2025.
  • At Marmato, first gold from the Lower Mine is expected in H2 2026, followed by a ramp-up through H1 2027 to roughly 200,000 ozpa.
  • At Toroparu, a prefeasibility study is anticipated in October 2026, with an FID targeted for November 2026. This will expand Aris Mining into Guyana—its second Latin American jurisdiction—adding meaningful geographic diversification.
  • Finally, a Soto Norte permit potentially around mid-2028 could remove what is widely regarded as the biggest overhang on the company. Apparently, inclusion in the S&P/TSX Composite Index doesn’t mean the business is spared from being misunderstood.

Risks

Permitting Soto Norte

The greatest uncertainty surrounding Aris Mining is whether the Soto Norte project can be permitted—and, if so, when.

ANLA previously rejected an earlier version of environmental permit application for Soto Norte. On January 19, 2021, ANLA denied all reconsideration requests on procedural grounds, citing insufficient information, thereby confirming its earlier order from October 2, 2020, which shelved the application. That was a setback although the operator (now Aris Mining) has the right to resubmit an EIA. In March 2025, the Colombian Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (Ministerio de Ambiente y Desarrollo Sostenible) established a Temporary Reserve Area (TRA) in the Santurbán region, where the Soto Norte project is located. The TRA allows the ministry to evaluate water resources in the area and temporarily suspends the issuance of environmental licenses for two years, until March 2027. At the time of the announcement, the market appeared to interpret the TRA as effectively rendering Soto Norte unpermittable.

Aris management has remained adamant that the project is permitable. During the consultation process leading up to the TRA, the company continued advancing environmental and technical studies for a redesigned PFS. To begin with, Soto Norte is neither in the Santurbán Páramo nor in its buffer zone, and Páramo water flows are separate from those of Soto Norte (Figure 20). In September 2025, Aris released a completely-redesigned PFS with a reduced scale and a smaller environmental footprint, mitigating main environmental concerns and making Soto Norte significantly more permittable. “The Project plans to reuse, recycle, and return approximately 96.5% of the Project’s total water requirements back to the environment, resulting in a net water use estimated at 3.5%.” Importantly, Aris plans to dedicate more than 20% of the 3,500-tpd mill capacity to local community miners, replicating the CMP model already used successfully at Segovia, replacing informal mills that pollute waterways with safe, licensed processing, and helping the Colombian government expand its tax base and collect revenues that would otherwise be difficult to realize.

On October 30, 2025, Aris Mining appointed Brigitte Baptiste, a prominent Colombian environmentalist, to its board of directors. She previously served as director of the Alexander von Humboldt Institute for Research on Biological Resources, which helped define the boundaries of the Santurbán Páramo. Baptiste would not have agreed to join the board if she believed Soto Norte was unpermittable.

Equally significant, on November 19, 2025, Aris announced that it had reached a settlement and termination agreement with the Colombian government to end the arbitration related to the Marmato and Segovia mines that Gran Colombia Gold Corp. had originally brought before the International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes. The settlement spared the Colombian government potentially $350 million in payments to Aris. In return, the government now has clear legal obligations to provide security for Aris’ operations, with commitments from the Ministry of Mines to help Aris form CMPs in the Marmato Upper mine, the army, the District Attorney’s office, and the environmental agency. The agreement is with the state of Colombia for a ten-year term, meaning its protections extend beyond the next presidential election on May 31, 2026. The settlement is believed to have created significant goodwill within government agencies.

Earlier, Aris Mining played a leading role in the rescue of 23 workers—18 from a CMP partner and 5 Aris employees—during the La Reliquia mine shaft collapse. This strengthened Aris’s reputation as a responsible, community-minded operator, helped Bogotá avoid bad press, and built goodwill as well.

The goodwill generated from the arbitration settlement, combined with the redesigned PFS, gave Woodyer the confidence to buy the remaining 49% of Soto Norte. Given his business acumen, Woodyer would never have spent $80 million on an asset he doubted could be permitted.

Aris plans to submit the environmental license application for Soto Norte to ANLA in May 2026. I believe Soto Norte is very likely to be permitted. If so, Aris Mining may have pulled off 2025's best transaction in the mining patch. In just 3 ½ years, Aris increased its stake in the 10.9-Moz Soto Norte from 0% to 100%, ending up owning what Eike Batista's mining company, AUX, purchased in 2011 for $1.5 billion:

  • 2022: Aris paid Mubadala $100 million in cash for a 20% operating interest and an option for an additional 30% through exploration capital commitment, implying a transaction metric of $46/oz.

  • May 2024: Aris bought an additional 31% for ~$89 million, paying $26/oz.

  • November 2025: Aris acquired the remaining 49% for $80 million and terminated the Mubadala stream, paying only $15/oz—an extremely low transactional metric.

Toroparu

On October 28, 2025, Aris released a positive PEA on the Toroparu project in Guyana. Judging from the encouraging results, Toroparu appears to carry relatively low execution risk. It is a high-grade (1.3–1.6 g/t gold) open-pit project requiring $820 million in initial capital expenditures, which Aris should be able to self-fund. Aris management is familiar with this geological type of gold deposit from their Endeavour days. Guyana is also one of the top-ranked mining jurisdictions in the world. Guyana is literally West African Birimian geology without the resource nationalism and coup d’états.

The Toroparu prefeasibility study will not be released until October 2026. However, there is good reason for optimism. Toroparu is very similar to the nearby Oko West deposit operated by G Mining Ventures (GMINF), albeit with a lower stripping ratio. In April 2025, G Mining released a robust feasibility study for Oko West, reporting an after-tax IRR of 27% and a 2.9-year payback.

There is a possibility that Aris management may be forced to sell the company too soon thus cutting short its organic growth and the value that would otherwise accrue to shareholders. A plausible acquirer may be Agnico Eagle Mines, which already owns approximately 14.99% of Collective Mining with participation rights in future financings. Agnico may attempt to acquire both Collective Mining and Aris Mining to consolidate the entire Guayabales–Marmato district (Figure 19). Buying Aris would give Agnico access to its CMP expertise, which could be important as Agnico enters Colombia as an operator. That said, I view an early sale as a low-probability outcome. Agnico is likely to wait until Guayabales is permitted before making a move, which is at least four to five years away. By that time, Aris will likely be a much larger and financially stronger company. It has already reduced net debt from $268 million at year-end 2024 to $64 million as of September 30, 2025, and is on track to have a very strong balance sheet.

Investor takeaways

I recently presented Jaguar Gold (OTCQX:JAGGF) as a still-undervalued gold producer. However, Jaguar is a special-situation investment play—a turnaround from an environmental disaster—so I don’t plan to stay in it much longer once the operational recovery is achieved.
